note: Many smc-index endpoint candidates were generated with systematic typos (getNomnator, getNominatorEarning, getPoolBooking, getpol) that make them artifact-like rather than natural queries and should be dropped. Several rate-limit and get-api-key candidates warrant expansion to both pages since plan/rate information appears equally in both. The introduction page candidates were sometimes claimed for queries that better fit more specific child pages (streaming overview, v3 overview).

Many candidates in this category target bare openapi stub pages that have no body content beyond a title and openapi path, making queries about concepts or how-tos inappropriate (ids 3, 6, 98 dropped). A recurring pattern is that v2-authentication and v3-authentication are nearly identical pages, causing many authentication-related queries to legitimately expand to both. Similarly, v2-errors and v3-errors both cover overlapping HTTP status codes, requiring expansion for generic error code queries.

The majority of candidates are exact or near-exact method name queries (with occasional typos) that map cleanly to their respective endpoint pages; these are strong keeps. Generic descriptive queries (e.g., 'wallet information API', 'toncenter masterchain API') are problematic because the category contains parallel v2 and v3 endpoints for the same concepts, creating genuine ambiguity. Conceptual/motivational queries ('why use X', 'what are shards') should be dropped as the corpus lacks dedicated concept pages to answer them.

Most candidates are well-formed endpoint-specific queries that map cleanly to thin OpenAPI stub pages; the main exceptions are error/troubleshooting queries (e.g., 'cannot read config', 'fee estimation returning zero') which should be dropped since no page in this category provides troubleshooting content. The v2/overview page is the only rich content page and appropriately absorbs broad how-to queries, while the rate-limit page (also in this category) is a better match than v2/overview for rate-limit-exceeded questions.

All pages in this batch are thin OpenAPI stubs with no body content beyond the endpoint path, making query evaluation rely entirely on endpoint name matching. Most candidates are well-formed with exact method names, path lookups, and typo variants that clearly identify a single endpoint, but generic descriptive queries (e.g., 'detailed block transaction list', 'get transactions', 'locate transaction') are systematically dropped because they match multiple sibling endpoints with equal relevance. The three try-locate-* endpoints are particularly prone to ambiguous queries that lack the result/source/plain distinction needed for a unique label.

Many candidates in this batch are slug-phrase or camelCase method name queries that trivially identify their target pages by string match; these were kept only when the query had some natural-language framing. Generic single-word queries like 'pagination', 'metadata', 'account states', and 'pending actions' were dropped as too ambiguous. Authentication-related queries frequently span both v2-authentication and v3-authentication pages which are near-identical in content, warranting expand verdicts for most of those candidates.

This batch covers OpenAPI stub pages for v3 toncenter endpoints where each page contains only an OpenAPI spec reference with no prose content, making many concept or troubleshooting queries inappropriate. Several endpoint types (estimate-fee, get-address-information, get-wallet-information, run-get-method) have near-identical counterparts in v2, so unversioned queries often expand to both versions. Generic single-word queries (wallet, fee, send, transactions) are consistently too ambiguous to keep.

This batch covers thin OpenAPI reference stub pages with no body content beyond the endpoint path, making verdicts rely entirely on query specificity. A recurring pattern is that path-artifact queries (containing '/api/v3/...') should be dropped, and several generic conceptual queries have no dedicated concept pages in this corpus. The shard-state group is unusual in having two near-identical pages (masterchainBlockShardState vs masterchainBlockShards) requiring expand verdicts for ambiguous queries.

The batch is dominated by endpoint-reference pages that serve as thin OpenAPI wrappers with no prose content, making conceptual or how-to queries (e.g., 'what are jetton wallets', 'understanding multisig transactions') poor candidates since the endpoint pages cannot answer conceptual questions. Several ambiguous queries covering multiple peer endpoints (e.g., 'toncenter v3 transfers', 'toncenter multisig API', 'toncenter nft API') were dropped because no single page clearly dominates. The GET vs POST distinction for the decode endpoint is meaningful and both variants are kept with their respective HTTP-method-specific pages.

Candidates 0-7 all target the decode-opcodes-and-bodies endpoint but the category has both GET and POST variants of /api/v3/decode; queries not explicitly specifying GET/POST should expand to both. Candidates 8-16 target the vesting contracts endpoint with mostly high-quality queries, but path-like queries (/api/v3/vesting) and overly generic concept queries (token lockup, token release schedule) should be dropped.

Many candidates are near-verbatim page title matches or typo variants that are still clear in intent; the main pattern of false labels involves queries that match a get-started page but equally match the corresponding howto page (e.g., networks, providers, connectors), warranting expand verdicts. A few queries conflate multiple concepts from different pages (id 39, id 95) or reference APIs that span several pages (id 12, id 21), making them genuinely ambiguous and warranting drops.

Many candidate queries had strong dual-page coverage between the get-started/* and howto/* sibling pages for the same feature (streaming, using-ui-widgets, networks), warranting expand verdicts. Several howto/howto index candidates were incorrectly claimed as the best answer when a more specific page directly addressed the query (read-balances, send-jettons, sign-data, onramp, nfts, staking). Generic single-word queries like 'appkit', 'providers', 'networks', and 'balances' were dropped due to insufficient specificity.

The category has a strong howto/reference split where many API symbols (useWatchBalance, useTransferJetton, etc.) appear in both the task-oriented howto pages and the API reference pages, requiring expand verdicts for these symbol-lookup queries. Several single-word queries (jettons, staking, streaming, Toncoin) that directly match page titles should be dropped as they measure string matching rather than relevance. The send-jettons and send-toncoin pages each have a dedicated component (SendJettonButton/SendTonButton) that is equally documented in use-ui-widgets, creating legitimate expand cases.

Most candidates for /applications/appkit/reference/reference are queries that would be better served by the actual reference pages (appkit or appkit-react), since the reference/reference page is just a thin index. Several candidates proposed for /applications/appkit/reference/appkit are well-targeted to that page's specific function documentation, particularly those referencing named API methods like getJettonsByAddress.

The /applications/sdks page is a comprehensive SDK listing that explicitly covers HTTP vs ADNL connection types, core libraries, wrappers, and emulators, with a detailed comparison table of named SDKs across multiple languages. All candidates reference specific content clearly present on this single page, and since this category contains only one page, every non-drop verdict is necessarily a keep. The candidates are well-formed queries that real users would type, referencing specific library names and concepts directly documented on the page.

The candidate set is well-structured overall, but several queries are too generic ('how to connect wallet', 'sendTransaction method', 'sign data message') to have a single clearly correct answer given overlapping coverage across the API reference, how-to, and get-started pages. A notable pattern is that ton_proof queries are split between FAQ and how-to/connect, but how-to/connect is consistently the better answer because it provides the full verification walkthrough rather than a brief mention.

The filter-wallets and send-transaction pages generate many specific-symbol queries (API identifiers, type names, error codes) that are easy to verify because those symbols appear only on one page. Several overview-level queries are too broad to own a single page and should be dropped. The troubleshooting page is well-targeted by its error-code queries, all of which have a single correct destination.

Many candidates propose api-reference as the correct answer for topics (useTonPay hook, TonPayButton) that are only briefly mentioned there but fully documented in dedicated pages; these required correction to payments-react or button-react. Several candidates covering server-side flows, testnet configuration, and manifest setup appropriately expand to multiple pages since those topics are repeated with equal depth across the integration guides. A pattern of generic how-to and testnet/mainnet questions were dropped because they match too many pages without a clear best answer.

All 10 candidates are well-scoped to the webhooks page, which is unusually rich in content covering configuration, payload structure, signature verification, retry behavior, idempotency, and troubleshooting. No candidates were ambiguous or cross-page; none referenced features absent from the corpus. The only mild concern is candidate 5 using 'ton connect' rather than 'TON Pay', but the `x-tonpay-signature` specificity anchors it firmly to the webhooks page.

Many candidates are platform-agnostic API queries (e.g., TONBridgeEventsHandler, wallet creation pattern, limit/offset pagination) that equally match both Android and iOS sibling pages, requiring expand verdicts. A significant portion of generic queries ('wallet address', 'connect request', 'how to transfer jettons') match too broadly across multiple pages and must be dropped. The browser-extension and native-web pages are thematically similar custodian-integration pages, causing several queries to expand across both.

The candidate set contains many high-quality, specific API-name queries that are strong relevance labels, but also a significant number of troubleshooting/error queries ('X not working', 'Y failing') that have no dedicated answer pages in this corpus and should be dropped. Several candidates claimed ios/webview but were better matched to ios/init or ios/events, and the TON Connect bridge topic is genuinely split between native-web and browser-extension pages, warranting expand verdicts for queries that don't specify a platform type.

Many candidates for the web/wallets page are platform-agnostic (mnemonic, private key, manage wallets) and should be expanded to all platform wallets pages; platform-specific APIs like WalletV5R1Adapter and custom signers correctly stay as keeps. The jettons and toncoin pages share structural similarities (balance check + monitoring + transfers from dApps + transfers in wallet service) causing some generic queries like 'jettons' or 'Toncoin balance' to be too broad. Several queries were generated as simple slug-recombinations or single-word terms that are unfit as relevance labels.

Most candidates are well-scoped to specific Blueprint subpages, with the api, benchmarks, config, coverage, and debug pages having nearly all queries correctly labeled. A cluster of overlap exists among deploy.mdx, develop.mdx, and first-smart-contract.mdx for shared concepts (send messages, get methods, createFromConfig), warranting several expand verdicts. Generic how-to queries like 'how to create smart contract' or 'contract source code generation' were dropped as they resolve equally well to multiple pages.

Most candidates are well-targeted and correctly labelled; the main issues are a few introduction-page candidates that claim the introduction as the answer for topics that have dedicated sibling technique pages (signing, sharding), and a handful of overly-generic queries (marketplace install, entry point) that are ambiguous across the category or match no specific page well enough. The testing/overview and testing/reference split causes several legitimate expand opportunities for types like SendMessageResult that appear substantively in both pages.

The candidates are well-formed and highly specific to their claimed pages, with most being direct matches to specific functions, sections, or concepts in the corresponding MDX files. Two candidates (ids 19 and 34) required expansion because the vanity and upgrades pages cross-reference each other conceptually. The signing page candidates were all legitimate with no generic or ambiguous queries.

This category has only one page, so all non-dropped candidates default to keep; the main risk is over-broad queries like 'TON blockchain' that would match the entire site. Most candidates are well-targeted to specific sections of the page (workchains, wallets, TVM, StateInit, messages, traces) and represent genuine user intents that the core-concepts page directly answers.

The Fift pages (deep-dive, whitepaper, overview) have significant content overlap — both the whitepaper and deep-dive cover arithmetic, defining words, loops, and conditionals, requiring many 'expand' verdicts. The FunC pages are more specialized (comments, asm-functions, built-ins, compiler-directives each own distinct content) yielding mostly 'keep' verdicts. A few candidates were dropped for being either too generic (comment documentation example) or misassigned to the wrong page (tilde operator belongs to expressions/operators, not compiler-directives).

The candidates are generally well-matched to their claimed pages; nearly all cookbook, known-issues, libraries, literals, and operators queries are unique best answers for their respective pages. The declarations-overview page is thin (just three bullet links) so several queries about its content are equally well answered by the linked child pages (functions, global-variables, compiler-directives), warranting expand verdicts. The functions page similarly has an asm-bodies section but the sibling asm-functions page is the deeper reference, so id=48 is expanded.

The majority of candidates are well-targeted and map correctly to their claimed pages; a handful of overview-claimed queries (ids 72-74) are better served by the more specific tooling or syntax-and-semantics pages. The FunC stdlib and dictionaries pages overlap for dictionary operation queries, warranting an expand verdict for id 34. Query id 77 is too generic to uniquely identify any single page.

Most candidates in this batch are well-targeted to specific content on their claimed pages, with the tep-examples page covering concrete TL-B schemas for NFT/Jetton/DNS standards and the tooling page covering specific library names and languages. The only drop is query 9 which mirrors the page title too closely. The tep-examples queries are notably strong because they reference specific identifiers (0xad01, VarUInteger 16, transfer_notification) that appear verbatim in the page content.

The candidate set is high quality overall with clear command-level queries that map well to dedicated pages; the main drop candidates are generic troubleshooting phrases (e.g., 'backup fails with error', 'liquid pool controller deployment fails'), overly broad architectural questions ('shard block producer', 'TON node setup tool'), or ambiguous topology phrases ('overlay network configuration'). Several settings like btcTeleportDisabled and auto_backup are documented in both the core settings table and their dedicated module pages, warranting expand verdicts.

The category has strong command-reference pages (pools, utilities, validator, wallet, collator) that are the unique best answers for specific command lookups, but step-by-step guides like run-validator and setup-mytonctrl overlap significantly for procedural queries such as hardware requirements, installation, and election participation. Several queries about staking concepts (commission, pool comparison) belong more precisely in the staking/ subcategory pages than in the mytonctrl command pages, requiring correct verdicts.

The Rust node section has many well-scoped pages with distinct topics, making most candidates straightforward keeps; the main expansion cases arise where configuration is split between a how-to page (node-config) and a reference page (node-config-ref). The staking section has significant content duplication between overview and detail pages (especially for liquid staking provider tables), requiring several expand verdicts. Typo/misspelling queries are generally kept when the intent is unambiguous and there is a clear best-match page, but dropped when the typo makes the intent genuinely uncertain (stacking vs staking) or when the query is so truncated it could match multiple pages.

Most candidates in this batch target the stake-calculation page and are well-formed, actionable queries that map cleanly to specific sections of that page. The single-nominator candidates are weaker: one is a near-duplicate of the page title, one is too generic ('singl nominator' typo fragment), and only id 0 represents a genuine user need. The 'staking' and 'steak calculation' candidates are clear drops due to excessive generality and slug-pattern origin respectively.

The candidates are well-targeted overall with most queries mapping cleanly to their claimed pages; the main issues are three single-word queries (jetton, payment, Toncoin) that are too generic to be useful relevance labels. The jettons page is distinctly security-focused with specific opcodes and attack patterns, making it easy to validate technical queries, while the toncoin and overview pages divide responsibilities clearly between architectural patterns and conceptual overview respectively.

Many single-word candidates (library, action, send, code, reserve, derive) were dropped as too generic. Several library-management queries needed expansion to include the serialization/library page alongside the change-library action page. The config page candidates were generally well-targeted, though the Catchain-consensus query was dropped because the config page only covers parameter values rather than explaining how the protocol works.

Most candidates are strong keep verdicts because the category has well-scoped pages with distinct topics and the queries match specific technical terms (TLB field names, mode flags, config parameters) that appear in exactly one page. The main drop patterns were: vague diagnostic questions whose answers live in linked whitepapers outside the category, a typo query (flat_gas_limite), and a yes/no question (id 59) where the page answer is 'no' rather than a page being the best resource. Several expand verdicts arise between ordinary-tx and phases pages since phases are documented in both files.

The candidates map cleanly to their claimed pages with high precision: most queries are specific enough (TL-B type names, opcode names, tag values) that only one page can answer them. A few queries about storage fees, account state proof verification, and descriptor byte calculation span two pages at similar depth and warrant expand verdicts. One query ('how to send message action phase') was corrected to the dedicated send-message actions page rather than the execution phases overview.

The web3 subsection (overview, ton-dns, ton-proxy, ton-sites, ton-storage) has significant content overlap — especially between ton-proxy and ton-sites, which share nearly identical sections on tonutils-reverse-proxy and domain assignment, requiring many 'expand' verdicts. The shards, status, system, and traces pages are highly self-contained with specific technical terminology matching their generated queries well, making most of those candidates straightforward 'keep' verdicts. One recurring challenge is distinguishing queries that belong primarily to the overview page versus dedicated sub-pages for DNS, proxy, sites, and storage.

Many candidates for the airdrop and mintless pages overlap significantly in their content, requiring expand verdicts where both the overview/how-it-works page and the deploy/API page cover the same topic with equal depth. The overview page (id 0-9) attracted several poorly scoped queries that are either too generic to be useful relevance labels or more accurately point to deeper pages. The API page and how-it-works page for jettons share many topics (message layouts, TL-B schemas) causing legitimate expand cases, while operation-specific how-to pages (burn, mint, find) are generally well-matched to their candidates.

Many candidates are high-quality and correctly labeled, but generic single-word or two-word queries (NFT, TON NFT, jetton) should be dropped as they match too many pages. Several candidates originally claimed the overview or how-it-works page when a more specific sibling page (comparison, transfer, api) is the better answer. The api pages for both jettons and NFTs are systematically under-utilized as expand targets when the query is about message opcodes or method specifications.

Most candidates are well-targeted at specific pages with technical specificity (opcode constants, TL-B schemas, function names) that confirms the claimed_correct page. A few generic single-word queries (e.g., 'NFT', 'vesting') were dropped as slug-equivalent or too broad. Some queries about shared concepts like TEP-62, seqno/replay protection, and subwallet IDs warranted 'expand' verdicts because multiple sibling pages cover those topics with equal depth.

Many candidates from the highload v3 guides (create, send-batch, send-single) overlap significantly in content such as createdAt timestamp, SendMode.PAY_GAS_SEPARATELY, and auto-deploy behavior, leading to several expand verdicts. The claimed_correct for several queries in the create guide incorrectly attributed mnemonic/keypair topics that belong to the mnemonics page. The specification page is the correct target for architectural and protocol-level queries (two-transaction pattern, query_id structure, timeout bits), even when those topics are briefly mentioned in how-to guides.

The candidate set covers five wallet sub-pages (performance, preprocessed-v2/interact, preprocessed-v2/specification, restricted, v4, v5, v5-api) with good specificity overall; the main weaknesses are queries for generic low-level TVM concepts (outlist, fwd_fee calculation) that lack a dedicated owner page in this category, and a small number of queries that are ambiguous between interact and specification pages for preprocessed-v2 because both pages share the 255-actions limit content.

Many candidates claimed correct for basic-syntax were actually better answered by dedicated sub-pages (functions-methods, variables, structures-fields, imports, contract-getters, message-sending), suggesting the generator over-attributed queries to the overview page. Several lazy-loading queries were correctly attributed to feature pages but should expand to include the dedicated lazy-loading page. The jetton-payload and changelog candidates were consistently well-attributed to their correct dedicated pages.

The candidate set is well-formed overall; typo variants (ids 9, 19, 29, 39, 49, 59, 69, 79, 89, 99) are consistently paired with their canonical page. The main correctness issues found were claims pointing to overview or tolk-vs-func pages when a dedicated feature page (contract-abi, message-sending, contract-storage) is the true best answer. Several conditions-loops candidates expand to pattern-matching or booleans because conditions-loops.mdx serves as an entry point that defers to those sibling pages for deeper coverage.

The candidates are well-matched to their claimed pages, as each page has a narrow, specific topic and the queries target features documented on those pages. A small number of typo queries (trow, imprt, mutete, strukt, matcn, addres, funtion, typ) are clearly intentional misspellings for robustness testing and are valid user queries. The lazy loading operator query (id 49) was the main correction needed, as the operators page only lists 'lazy' in the priority table while the actual documentation is on the lazy-loading page.

The candidates are mostly well-matched to their claimed pages, which contain dedicated sections for nearly every queried topic. A few candidates claimed list-of-types as the best answer when a more specific sibling page (aliases, overall-tvm-stack, overall-serialization) directly covers the topic. The serialization and TVM-stack pages are frequently eligible as expand targets alongside their dedicated type pages, since those pages repeat key serialization facts.

Many candidates in this batch are high quality with specific method names, code identifiers, and technical details that uniquely identify their target pages. The main patterns requiring non-keep verdicts are: generic two-word or title-identical queries (ids 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80) which should be dropped, and a handful of cases where a sibling page covers the topic equally well or better (notably lazy-loading for lazy matching, unknown for cast-complex-values, and several nullable/unions overlaps).

The candidate set is well-structured overall, with each page generating 10 queries including one typo variant. The main issues are: (1) a few register-related queries were claimed for continuations or initialization when the dedicated registers page is the better answer; (2) the retracer/txtracer tools overlap significantly for debugging and tracing queries, warranting several expansions; (3) id 99 is an exact duplicate of id 90 and should be dropped.

All 30 candidates target the three tools pages (ton-decompiler, tvm-explorer, txtracer); the non-tools pages in this category (instructions, overview, registers, etc.) are entirely unrepresented in this batch. Several queries are near-duplicates or misspellings of each other (ids 0/9, 15/19, 22/28), and one candidate (id 14, 'TVM instructions') was incorrectly assigned to tvm-explorer when the dedicated instructions page is the clear winner.

Most candidates are well-targeted to the correct whitepaper page, with the main exceptions being candidates 11 (Nikolai Durov as sole term is ambiguous across all pages) and 8/38 (generic phrases not uniquely answerable). Several candidates (13, 20) are duplicates targeting different pages, which is notable: 'TON blockchain specification' correctly maps to tblkch rather than overview. The overview page has a distinct role as a navigation/index page for PDFs and translations, so queries specifically about that meta-content (translations, PDF downloads, gram/nanogram) are well-matched to it.

Most candidates are well-matched to this single page, which is a comprehensive style guide covering voice, tone, content types, formatting, prerequisites, and writing mechanics. The two drops are a generic phrase (id 0) that would match any style guide, and a title-reconstruction query (id 8) that measures string matching rather than relevance. The remaining queries map clearly to specific named sections of the document.

The candidate set is generally well-labeled, with most queries matching their claimed pages; the main correction pattern is queries that target specific sub-pages (npm install, script install, API endpoints) being incorrectly claimed against the overview page. Several generic queries like 'analytics setup', 'analytics integration', and troubleshooting queries without dedicated pages should be dropped to avoid training the search engine on ambiguous or unanswerable labels. The preparation and managing-integration pages have significant content overlap around the TON Builders API key flow, warranting expand verdicts for queries that mention both topics.

The candidates are generally well-matched to their pages, with one clear mislabeling: ID 1 'AppRoot component telegram-ui' was claimed for getting-started but platform-and-palette is the dedicated AppRoot component reference page (its actual title). The avatar reference page has a thin outline but its prose description is sufficient to validate all avatar-related queries. The overview and platform-and-palette pages handle distinct concerns (what-is vs. how-theming-works) cleanly, with no significant overlap requiring expansions.

Most candidates are well-formed definitional queries that appropriately map to a glossary page, but several overly generic or technical-depth queries (blockchain definition, smart contract, FunC language, proof-of-stake) should be dropped because the full corpus almost certainly contains dedicated concept pages that would be better answers than a one-liner glossary entry.

The candidates are generally well-matched to their claimed pages with two clear mislabelings: split-key architecture (id 4) belongs to wallets.mdx not mcp.mdx, and ton/mcp tools listing (id 12) belongs to mcp.mdx not overview.mdx. The agentic wallets vs overview boundary (id 11) also required correction since wallets.mdx is the dedicated authoritative source for that concept. Several overview-page candidates (ids 13-17) are legitimately unique to that page due to its documentation-AI-surface content.

Most candidates are well-targeted to their claimed pages, with each component page (aside, filetree, image) generating queries that precisely match its documented props and use-cases; the three bare page-title queries (ids 0, 9, 18) were dropped as title matches. The overview and style-guide pages attract broader conceptual queries without significant overlap, and the few borderline expand cases (e.g., Callout/Aside relationship) were kept rather than expanded due to the high threshold required.

This single-page category is well-scoped and most candidates map cleanly to its three sections (Telegram support, bug bounty, GitHub). The two dropped queries were either a near-duplicate of the page title with a typo ('suport get help') or an overly generic phrase ('get help TON') that provides no search signal beyond string overlap with the title.

The candidates are generally well-matched to their claimed pages, with most being precise queries about specific tools, SDKs, or features covered on a single page. The typo variants (analitics, brdiges, explrer, oricle, etc.) are legitimate as they still uniquely identify the target page within this category. A handful of queries like 'data providers' and 'status' are too generic to uniquely identify a single page. The Pyth and RedStone oracle pages share enough overlap on the general concept of price feed integration that a few cross-oracle queries warranted expansion.

The from-ethereum page is a strong, dedicated comparison page whose queries were mostly correctly labeled, with a few outliers (ton-connect, get-methods) where the real content lives elsewhere in the corpus. The glossary candidates were generally well-labeled but a couple (toncoin vs bitcoin comparison, WAGMI as market terminology) had label quality issues. The more-tutorials page was mostly correctly labeled, though sandbox-testing intent points outside this category.

This category contains a single introductory page that serves as a navigation hub; most queries about specific technical features (sharding, Acton, AppKit) are only briefly mentioned with links pointing elsewhere, making those queries poor fits. The page is well-suited for high-level 'what is TON' and 'how to start' queries, but overly generic or feature-specific queries should be dropped.

Most dropped candidates target streaming API or AppKit documentation that is only tangentially referenced here; this page is primarily an adoption guide for sub-second finality. The strongest keepers are queries that name sub-second finality explicitly or ask about concepts (indexer lag, transaction statuses) that the page treats as primary topics rather than passing references.

Most candidates are well-formed and map cleanly to named sections within the toolset overview page. Two queries were dropped: one for being a generic how-to with no dedicated page in the category, and one for being overly broad and matching many pages across the corpus. The remaining candidates have strong keyword overlap with explicit section content rather than just page-level topics.

The tonkeeper and web (wallet.ton.org) pages are near-duplicate in structure and content — both cover create wallet, generate key, switch to testnet, add funds, deploy code, and verify wallet version — leading to many expand verdicts where both pages equally answer a query. The addresses-workflow page is tightly scoped to address format and validation logic, making it easy to keep those candidates. Several get-coins queries were easy keeps, but a few were dropped due to querying for information (timeframes, deploy semantics) not actually covered in the corpus.
